macro_segments.title: Introduction to Masechta Ketubot: Overview of Topics, Structure, and Core Concepts

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context: The three difficult masechtot (Eruvin, Niddah, Yevamos); Ketubot as the 'mini Shas'
Primary topics of Ketubot: Nisuin (cohabitation) vs. Kiddushin (legal marriage); the distinction and timing
Three major parts of the Ketubah: reciprocal obligations, lump-sum payment (200 or 100 zuz), and dowry documentation
Broader themes and diverse Shas concepts in Ketubot: kam lei b'derabba minei, witness testimony, and rape/seduction cases
Student question about dowry listing in modern ketubah; lecturer's response on pro forma language

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Wedding Days: Virgins on Wednesday, Widows/Divorcees on Thursday, and Initial Questions

micro_segments.title: Mishnah text: betulah niset l'yom ha-revi'i (virgin married on Wednesday); almanah l'yom hachamishi (widow on Thursday)
Explanation of ta'anat betulim (claim of non-virginity): financial consequences (200 vs. 100 zuz) and suspicion of adultery
Purpose of Wednesday wedding: enabling the groom to bring the claim to beit din on Thursday morning
Initial question: Why not Sunday, so he could go to court on Monday? The gemarah will address this
Rashi vs. Tosafot debate on the mechanism of ta'anat betulim: witnesses vs. shvuyah anafsheh

macro_segments.title: Application to Mezonot (Food Obligation): When Does the Husband's Support Begin?

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Higia zman v'lo nisu—once the designated wedding date arrives, the husband must provide food and trumah for the wife
Practical case: If the year expires on a Sunday (not Wednesday), does the husband pay from Sunday to Wednesday?
Answer: The real wedding date is the first Wednesday after one year; he pays only from Wednesday onward

macro_segments.title: Application to Mezonot (Food Obligation): When Does the Husband's Support Begin? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's challenge: This makes the first mishnah (about ta'anat betulim) dependent on a later mishnah (about mezonot); the gemarah clarifies this is acceptable

macro_segments.title: Gemarah's Response to the Mishnah: Takanat Chachamim and the Reason for Wednesday (Not Sunday)

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's objection: Making a mishnah with stated reason dependent on a later mishnah without stated reason
Gemarah's response: Both mishnahs are authentic; clarifying the relationship between the two teachings
Corrected teaching: The reason for Wednesday is rabbinic institution (takanat chachamim) to ensure the groom spends three days preparing the wedding feast
Why Wednesday and not Sunday: The rabbis wanted the groom to invest effort over three days (Sunday–Tuesday) in preparing the seudah
Implicit consequence: The gemarah treats the mishnah as rabbinic demand (not mere custom) that strengthens the claim of when obligations begin

macro_segments.title: Exceptions and Complications: When Obligations Do NOT Begin (Ones—Force and Circumstance)

micro_segments.title: Question: Does the obligation to pay mezonot begin automatically on the wedding date, or only if someone is to blame for delay?
Case of groom illness: Does he pay mezonot from the appointed date if he got sick and the wedding was postponed?
Case of bride illness: Does the groom still pay if she became ill? Debate over whose misfortune it represents
Case of bride becoming niddah: Does the groom pay if the bride became niddah (menstruating) on the wedding date?
Rav Achai's reading: The mishnah frames it as 'women were not married,' implying it was not willful postponement by the woman
Rav Ashi's position: Any ones (force/circumstance out of control) exempts from payment; only willful postponement by groom triggers obligation

macro_segments.title: Digression on Prenuptial Agreements in Modern Practice and Their Halachic Parallel

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's historical parallel: In Talmudic times, many fights occurred over ketubah terms; get married early Wednesday to resolve disputes in time
Lecturer's anecdote: Groom refusing to sign prenup at wedding due to parents' nasty divorce
Contemporary application: Insist on signed and notarized prenup two weeks before wedding to avoid last-minute disputes over ketubah

macro_segments.title: Transition to Gittin (Divorce): Contrasting Ones in Mezonot vs. Gittin; the Condition-Met Problem

micro_segments.title: Rava's principle: Ein ones b'gittin—the fact that something was beyond one's control is not a factor in gittin law
Case source: A man gives conditional divorce (harei zeh gitcha if I don't return in 12 months) and dies within that period
Analysis: The condition was met (he didn't return), but the get is invalid because ein get l'achar misah (no divorce after death)
Gemarah's difficulty: Does the mishnah imply ones is a factor (so if he got sick, it's like he returned, so no get)? Or only that death prevents get?
Preview: Social policy reason why ones cannot be a factor: we would never know if conditions were met
Gemarah's resolution: The mishnah teaches only that get fails after death, not whether ones affects condition-met analysis

topical_tags.term: Higia zman v'lo nisu (time came and they did not marry)
Shvuyah anafsheh chaticha d'issura (bound by one's own claim)
Ein ones b'gittin (ones is not a factor in gittin)
Ein get l'achar misah (no divorce after death)
Kam lei b'derabba minei (more serious violation outweighs fine)
